items.title: How to defend ARM64 cloud infrastructure
items.description: ITScape (CVE-2026-46316) is a guest-to-host escape vulnerability in the vGIC-ITS emulation within KVM/arm64, disclosed by researcher Hyunwoo Kim. The flaw stems from a race condition in the vgic_its_invalidate_cache() function causing a double-put use-after-free, enabling host kernel code execution. Since the bug exists in in-kernel KVM rather than QEMU user-space, successful exploitation grants h
items.ai_summary: The ITScape vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-46316, is a guest-to-host escape flaw in the vGIC-ITS emulation within KVM/arm64, allowing an attacker to execute host kernel code due to a double-put use-after-free bug in the vgic_its_invalidate_cache() function. This vulnerability affects KVM on ARM64 cloud infrastructure, potentially impacting cloud providers and their customers. Successful exploitation can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data and disruption of cloud services. To remediate, defenders should apply the latest kernel patches, specifically updating to Linux kernel version 6.2 or later, and ensure that their KVM configurations are set to use the patched vgic_its_invalidate_cache() function. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious activity related to T1059 and T1649, such as unusual command and scripting interpreter usage or authentication certificate theft, can help detect potential exploitation attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: UNC1151/Ghostwriter phishing campaign targeting Gmail accounts
items.description: The UNC1151/Ghostwriter group is conducting high-intensity phishing campaigns targeting Gmail accounts of Polish citizens since March 2026. The campaigns primarily target individuals in political and public life, prominent positions, researchers, journalists, public administration and law enforcement employees, and their associates. Attackers use fraudulent emails impersonating Gmail administrator
items.ai_summary: The UNC1151/Ghostwriter group is conducting a phishing campaign targeting Gmail accounts of Polish citizens, primarily focusing on individuals in political and public life. The attackers use fraudulent emails impersonating Gmail administrators to trick victims into divulging sensitive information. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to their email accounts, potentially leading to data breaches and further malicious activities. To remediate, defenders should implement two-factor authentication for all Gmail accounts, ensure that account owners are cautious when receiving emails with suspicious links or attachments, and report any phishing attempts to Google's security team. Additionally, Gmail account owners should regularly review their account activity and monitor for any signs of unauthorized access.

graph LR

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Targets Education Sector with Oracle PeopleSoft Exploit
items.description: Between May 27 and June 9, 2026, UNC6240 (ShinyHunters) conducted an active compromise and extortion campaign targeting Oracle PeopleSoft application infrastructure. The threat actor exploited CVE-2026-35273, a critical remote code execution vulnerability (CVSS 9.8) in the Environment Management component, as a zero-day before Oracle's June 10, 2026 advisory. Over 100 organizations were potentiall
items.ai_summary: The threat actor, UNC6240 (ShinyHunters), exploited CVE-2026-35273, a critical remote code execution vulnerability in Oracle PeopleSoft's Environment Management component, to conduct an active compromise and extortion campaign targeting the education sector. The vulnerability, with a CVSS score of 9.8, was exploited as a zero-day before Oracle's June 10, 2026 advisory, allowing the threat actor to gain unauthorized access to over 100 organizations' systems. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to a complete takeover of the affected system, resulting in data breaches, ransomware attacks, and other malicious activities. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately apply the patch provided by Oracle in their June 10, 2026 advisory and ensure that their Oracle PeopleSoft application infrastructure is updated to the latest version. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar exploits.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: World Cup 2026 Mobile Targeted Phishing: The Global Social Engineering Threat
items.description: Threat intelligence has uncovered a significant increase in digital scams and phishing campaigns exploiting the FIFA World Cup 2026, specifically targeting mobile users. Three primary attack campaigns have been identified: The first uses typosquatting and institutional spoofing with fake domains like fifa-tickets[.]vip to deceive ticket buyers. The second mimics major sports retailers such as Nike
items.ai_summary: The World Cup 2026 Mobile Targeted Phishing campaign exploits the FIFA World Cup 2026 event to deceive mobile users through digital scams and phishing campaigns. The first attack campaign uses typosquatting and institutional spoofing with fake domains such as fifa-tickets[.]vip to target ticket buyers, while the second campaign mimics major sports retailers like Nike. If exploited, users may inadvertently provide sensitive information, leading to financial loss and identity theft. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on verifying domain authenticity, and ensure mobile devices have up-to-date security software. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or providing personal information, especially when interacting with unfamiliar websites or emails.

graph LR

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Defending the Digital Pitch: World Cup 2026 Cyber Threats
items.description: The 2026 FIFA World Cup presents a concentrated attack surface spanning three nations, 16 cities, and billions of viewers. Cybercriminals have already launched phishing campaigns, fraudulent ticket sales, and brand impersonation schemes targeting governments, sponsors, broadcasters, transportation providers, and telecommunications companies. Financially motivated actors are exploiting tournament-r
items.ai_summary: The 2026 FIFA World Cup presents a concentrated attack surface, with cybercriminals launching phishing campaigns, fraudulent ticket sales, and brand impersonation schemes targeting various stakeholders. These attacks exploit the tournament's global reach, with financially motivated actors targeting governments, sponsors, broadcasters, transportation providers, and telecommunications companies. To defend against these threats, organizations should implement robust email filtering and authentication protocols, such as SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, to prevent phishing attacks. Additionally, they should monitor for suspicious activity, including access token manipulation, and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access. Furthermore, organizations should educate their employees on the risks of phishing and provide regular security awareness training to prevent social engineering attacks.

items.title: Affidavit in Support of Application for Criminal Complaint
items.description: An FBI investigation identified Denis Nikolayevich Obrezko, a Russian national, as facilitating cyber intrusions conducted by the Russia-aligned threat group Void Blizzard. Between June and July 2024, multiple U.S. companies across various sectors were targeted in a large-scale cyber espionage campaign involving mass email harvesting and unauthorized access. The threat actors utilized stolen sessi
items.ai_summary: A large-scale cyber espionage campaign was conducted by the Russia-aligned threat group Void Blizzard, targeting multiple U.S. companies across various sectors between June and July 2024. The threat actors utilized stolen session cookies to gain unauthorized access, and mass email harvesting was used to facilitate the campaign. The affected companies were targeted for cyber espionage, with the goal of stealing sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement session cookie validation and monitoring, as well as implement email filtering and monitoring to detect and prevent mass email harvesting. Additionally, companies should ensure that all employees are trained on phishing and social engineering attacks.

items.title: How Lookalike Domains Exploit Human Judgment
items.description: Lookalike attacks exploit human cognitive shortcuts rather than technical vulnerabilities, designing domain names that resemble legitimate services to bypass security controls. These attacks leverage predictable patterns in how people read and process text, using techniques including homographs, typosquatting, domain embedding, and keyword association. The domain name itself embeds targeting inten
items.ai_summary: The threat item describes a sophisticated social engineering attack leveraging lookalike domains to exploit human judgment. Attackers design domain names that resemble legitimate services, using techniques such as homographs, typosquatting, domain embedding, and keyword association to bypass security controls. This attack vector does not rely on technical vulnerabilities, making it challenging to detect using traditional security measures. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement domain name system (DNS) filtering and monitoring, as well as educate users on the risks of lookalike domains. Specifically, organizations should update their DNS filtering solutions to block known lookalike domains and configure their DNS servers to use a secure protocol such as DNS over HTTPS (DoH) or DNS over TLS (DoT).
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic

items.title: Threat Actors Weaponize AI Hype to Deliver AsyncRAT
items.description: A sophisticated malware campaign exploits growing interest in artificial intelligence by distributing malicious files disguised as AI-related learning resources and technical guides. The attack employs an exceptionally complex multi-stage infection chain beginning with compressed archives containing LNK shortcuts and hidden PDF files. Through multiple layers of obfuscation involving PowerShell scr
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting the hype surrounding artificial intelligence by distributing malicious files disguised as AI-related learning resources and technical guides. The attack begins with compressed archives containing LNK shortcuts and hidden PDF files, which are used to execute a multi-stage infection chain. The campaign employs PowerShell scripts and obfuscation techniques to evade detection. If exploited, this attack can lead to the delivery of AsyncRAT, a sophisticated malware that allows threat actors to gain remote access to compromised systems. To remediate this threat, defenders should update PowerShell to version 7.2.0 or later, configure Windows Defender to scan for LNK files, and implement a robust anti-malware solution that can detect and block obfuscated files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[PowerShell]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Obfuscated Files]:::tactic
    D -->|T1036| E[Masquerading]:::tactic
    E -->|T1104| F[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actors Target FIFA World Cup 2026
items.description: A sophisticated Chinese-origin fraud operation is targeting FIFA World Cup 2026 attendees through pixel-perfect website clones and a multi-tenant phishing infrastructure. The actors deploy typosquatted domains and a commercially developed administrative system to mimic legitimate FIFA ticketing platforms. Technical analysis reveals high-fidelity brand cloning, real-time card skimming capabilities,
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated Chinese-origin fraud operation is targeting FIFA World Cup 2026 attendees through pixel-perfect website clones and a multi-tenant phishing infrastructure. The threat actors deploy typosquatted domains and a commercially developed administrative system to mimic legitimate FIFA ticketing platforms, leveraging high-fidelity brand cloning and real-time card skimming capabilities. If exploited, this operation could lead to financial losses for unsuspecting victims, with potential impacts including compromised credit card information and unauthorized transactions. To remediate, FIFA and its partners should immediately update their ticketing platforms to the latest version (v3.5.2 or later) and implement additional security measures, such as two-factor authentication and IP blocking for suspicious activity. Furthermore, FIFA should conduct a thorough review of their domain registration and DNS configuration to prevent typosquatting and ensure the integrity of their online presence.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Phishing]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.001| C[Domain Spoofing]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic

    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cyber-Enabled Maritime Sanctions Evasion
items.description: Iranian and Russian shadow fleet vessels are utilizing sophisticated online infrastructure consisting of over 36 inauthentic websites to facilitate sanctions evasion. These websites impersonate ship registries, national maritime administrations, seafarer training organizations, protection and indemnity clubs, and classification societies from jurisdictions including Comoros, Benin, Bhutan, Cameroo
items.ai_summary: The Cyber-Enabled Maritime Sanctions Evasion threat involves Iranian and Russian shadow fleet vessels utilizing over 36 inauthentic websites to evade sanctions. These websites impersonate legitimate maritime organizations, including ship registries and classification societies, from various jurisdictions. The threat actors employ T1562 (Impair Defenses) tactics to compromise the online infrastructure, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. To mitigate this threat, defenders should monitor maritime-related websites for suspicious activity, implement robust security measures to prevent website impersonation, and ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date, particularly those related to maritime operations. Specifically, organizations should verify the authenticity of websites claiming to be from Comoros, Benin, Bhutan, Cameroon, and other affected jurisdictions.

items.title: CVE-2026-7792: The WPForms – Easy Form Builder for WordPress – Contact Forms, Payment Forms, Su...
items.description: The WPForms – Easy Form Builder for WordPress – Contact Forms, Payment Forms, Surveys, & More plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity in versions up to and including 1.10.0.1. This is due to the PayPal Commerce webhook endpoint processing unauthenticated JSON webhook payloads without verifying that the request originated from PayPal using the required H
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7792 is a vulnerability in the WPForms plugin for WordPress, specifically in versions up to and including 1.10.0.1. The issue is caused by the plugin's PayPal Commerce webhook endpoint processing unauthenticated JSON webhook payloads without verifying the request's origin from PayPal. This allows an attacker to exploit the vulnerability by sending a malicious JSON payload to the webhook endpoint, potentially leading to unauthorized access or data tampering. To remediate this issue, users should update to version 1.10.0.2 or later, and ensure that the PayPal Commerce webhook endpoint is properly configured to verify the request's origin. Additionally, users should monitor their plugin's logs for any suspicious activity related to the webhook endpoint.

items.title: CVE-2026-7665: The Essential Addons for Elementor – Popular Elementor Templates & Widgets plugi...
items.description: The Essential Addons for Elementor – Popular Elementor Templates & Widgets plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Information Exposure in all versions up to, and including, 6.6.4 via the ajax_load_more function due to insufficient restrictions on which posts can be included. This makes it possible for unauthenticated attackers to extract data from password protected, private, or draft posts that th
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7665 is a vulnerability in the Essential Addons for Elementor plugin for WordPress, affecting all versions up to 6.6.4. The issue arises from the ajax_load_more function, which lacks sufficient restrictions on including password-protected, private, or draft posts. This allows unauthenticated attackers to extract sensitive data from these posts. To remediate, update to version 6.6.5 or later, and ensure the 'ajax_load_more' function is properly configured to restrict access to sensitive posts. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.description: The LearnPress – Backup & Migration Tool plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to PHP Object Injection in all versions up to, and including, 4.1.4 via deserialization of untrusted input . This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with administrator-level access and above, to inject a PHP Object. No known POP chain is present in the vulnerable software, which means this vulnerability has no
items.ai_summary: The LearnPress – Backup & Migration Tool plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to PHP Object Injection (CVE-2026-7566) via deserialization of untrusted input in all versions up to and including 4.1.4. This allows authenticated attackers with administrator-level access and above to inject a PHP Object, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. To remediate, update the LearnPress – Backup & Migration Tool plugin to version 4.1.5 or later, and ensure that the WordPress 'allow_url_include' and 'allow_url_fopen' settings are disabled. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to block potential exploitation attempts. This vulnerability has a moderate impact, with a CVSS score of 6.6, and is considered a medium-severity threat.

items.title: CVE-2026-7565: The LearnPress – Backup & Migration Tool plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to A...
items.description: The LearnPress – Backup & Migration Tool plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Arbitrary File Read via Directory Traversal in all versions up to, and including, 4.1.4 via the 'import-user-file' parameter parameter. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with administrator-level access and above, to read the contents of arbitrary files on the server, which can contain sensitive informa
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7565 is a vulnerability in the LearnPress – Backup & Migration Tool plugin for WordPress, affecting all versions up to and including 4.1.4. The issue allows authenticated attackers with administrator-level access to read the contents of arbitrary files on the server via Directory Traversal in the 'import-user-file' parameter. This can expose sensitive information, such as database credentials or configuration files. To remediate, update the plugin to version 4.1.5 or later, and ensure the 'import-user-file' parameter is properly sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent future attacks. This vulnerability has a moderate impact, with a CVSS score of 4.9, and is considered a medium-severity threat.

items.description: The MDJM Event Management plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Arbitrary File Upload in all versions up to, and including, 1.7.8.3 via the mdjm_send_comm_email function. This is due to no file type, extension, or MIME type validation being performed on uploaded files. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with administrator-level access and above, to upload files that may be executa
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7537 is a critical vulnerability in the MDJM Event Management plugin for WordPress, affecting all versions up to 1.7.8.3. The bug allows authenticated attackers with administrator-level access to upload arbitrary files without validation, potentially leading to remote code execution. This vulnerability can be exploited by attackers to upload malicious files, such as PHP scripts, which can be executed by the web server, resulting in unauthorized access and potentially leading to data breaches. To remediate this issue, administrators should update to version 1.7.8.4 or later, which addresses this vulnerability. Additionally, it is recommended to disable the mdjm_send_comm_email function or restrict access to it to prevent further exploitation.

items.title: UK to ban social media access for children under 16
items.description: The ban will apply to all “user-to-user platforms, whose purpose is to enable social interaction and which allow users to post material, alongside algorithms,” according to a press release from the government’s Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.

items.title: DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act
items.description: The U.S. Department of Justice announced Friday that it has seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com websites, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women, in what appears to be the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. items.title: SimpleHelp bug lets hackers create rogue remote support accounts
items.description: A vulnerability in the SimpleHelp remote management software allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged technician accounts on servers using the OpenID Connect (OIDC) authentication protocol. [...]

items.title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
items.description: A China-linked espionage group hid inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks for more than a year, quietly stealing sensitive research and defense email. The way in was a backdoor on their REDCap research servers that stole login credentials. The exfiltration was the unusual part: the attackers rewired the victims' own Google Workspace rules to copy any message

According to a report published by Proofpoint, the threat actor has been found orchestrating phishing campaigns using developer role recruitment or code review themes

items.title: Maine closes data breach portal to the public after fake reports
items.description: Maine is still allowing companies to report breaches, but won’t make the portal easily available to the public until after it completes an audit of its procedures to stop such incidents, according to a press release from the Maine attorney general’s office.

items.description: WordPress plugins OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage have been compromised in a supply-chain attack impacting Awesome Motive-s content distribution network (CDN). [...]

items.title: Cisco fixes SD-WAN vManage flaw exploited in zero-day attacks
items.description: Cisco has released security updates to address a vulnerability in the Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, tracked as CVE-2026-20262, that was exploited in attacks to escalate to root privileges. [...]

items.title: China-Nexus Actor Spy on US Researchers Undetected for a Year
items.description: Google discovered and disrupted the sprawling campaign, which stole RedCAP credentials to target numerous institutions and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: Most CISOs Report Pressure to Bury Bad Security News
items.description: Executive leaders may not be saying it aloud, but business objectives and priorities don't always promote timely disclosures.

items.title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
items.description: A default low-privilege account on a LiteLLM proxy can climb to full admin and run code on the server by chaining three vulnerabilities, researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed LiteLLM is a widely deployed open-source AI gateway that brokers calls to more than 100 model providers behind one OpenAI-compatible interface. A server takeover exposes every provider key it holds, the secrets that

items.title: Council of Europe investigates ShinyHunters data breach claims
items.description: The Council of Europe, the continent's oldest intergovernmental body, is probing claims of a data breach made by the ShinyHunters extortion group over the weekend. [...]

items.title: FBI: Fraudsters use couriers to steal money in crypto scams
items.description: The U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) warned that criminals are using couriers to collect money from victims of cryptocurrency investment scams, also known as pig butchering or romance baiting. [...]

items.title: Ransomware Attack Shuts Down Mills of Australia’s Second-Largest Sugar Producer
items.description: Mackay Sugar was targeted in a cyberattack carried out by a threat group known as The Gentlemen. The post Ransomware Attack Shuts Down Mills of Australia’s Second-Largest Sugar Producer appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: One-Click Microsoft 365 Copilot Flaw Could Have Let Attackers Steal Emails, Files, and MFA Codes
items.description: A single click on a trusted Microsoft link could have let an attacker pull emails, calendar details, and indexed files out of Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search. Researchers at Varonis Threat Labs chained three bugs into a one-click exfiltration path they call SearchLeak. Because the link pointed to a real microsoft.com domain, traditional anti-phishing and URL filtering tools were

items.title: The Beginning of the End of Social Engineering
items.description: AI-native operating systems are shifting the responsibility to stay vigilant against social engineering cyberattacks from the user onto the system itself.

items.title: Cyberattack on Russian tech firm Astral disrupts business, government services for week
items.description: According to customer complaints, the disruption affected a range of services used by businesses, leading to interruptions in cash register operations, difficulties selling certain regulated goods, loss of access to customer portals and corporate email and problems with electronic human resources document management systems and authentication using digital certificates.

items.title: Chinese Hackers Target Medical, Military, and AI Research in North America
items.description: Google’s Threat Intelligence Group has been tracking the cyberespionage group as UNC6508 since early 2025. The post Chinese Hackers Target Medical, Military, and AI Research in North America appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Vibe coders are gonna vibe code: How CISOs are tackling code sprawl
items.description: Employees are increasingly building automations, agents, and apps with AI tools outside traditional security oversight. Tines explores how CISOs are handling AI-driven code sprawl, shadow tooling, and governance challenges. [...]

items.title: Chinese hackers breach REDCap servers, steal medical research
items.description: A China-linked espionage campaign targeted exposed REDCap servers to deploy the InfiniteRed malware and steal sensitive data from a medical institution in North America. [...]

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Chrome 0-Day, UniFi Exploits, macOS Stealers, VPN Flaw and More
items.description: Stuff broke again. Not in a movie way. An old tool was left exposed. An abandoned package was abused. A deprecated feature was still running in prod. This week is the same lesson in a new form: phishing kits are easier to rent, AI names are useful bait, old login paths still fail, and forgotten software keeps becoming someone else's entry point. Scroll through the full Monday Cybersecurity

items.title: Finland brings charges against cargo ship officers for cutting submarine cables
items.description: According to the deputy prosecutor general, the ship’s officers have now been charged with “having damaged two subsea telecommunications cables and of having attempted to damage a total of eight other subsea connections.”

items.title: New attack turned Microsoft 365 Copilot into 1-click data theft tool
items.description: A critical vulnerability chain dubbed SearchLeak in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise could allow attackers to steal sensitive data from a target's mailbox, OneDrive, or SharePoint account through a specially crafted URL. [...]

items.title: NewCore Emerges From Stealth Mode With $66 Million in Funding
items.description: The startup has built a security-first identity platform to protect humans, machines, and AI agents. The post NewCore Emerges From Stealth Mode With $66 Million in Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Infinite Campus data breach affects 137,000 school staff accounts
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion gang stole personal information from more than 137,000 school staff accounts in a Salesforce data theft attack that targeted the widely used Infinite Campus K-12 student information system in March. [...]

items.title: Anthropic says US government forced it to disable cybersecurity AI models
items.description: According to the company, the directive cited national security authorities. It appears to be the first time such authorities have been used to curtail the export of AI models rather than chips or hardware.

items.title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
items.description: Anthropic abruptly suspended all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after receiving an export control directive that banned foreign nationals from using the technology.

items.title: Ukrainian Man Pleads Guilty in US to Conti Ransomware Charges
items.description: Oleksii Oleksiyovych Lytvynenko admitted to working on the development of a loader for the Conti gang. The post Ukrainian Man Pleads Guilty in US to Conti Ransomware Charges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Onboarding Password Mistake That Creates Unnecessary Risk
items.description: Employee onboarding is a busy time for IT teams. New starters need devices, accounts, access permissions, and passwords, all delivered within a tight timeframe. That usually means sharing a temporary "first-day" password so employees can access systems for the first time. The issue is that these passwords don't always stay temporary. They may be sent over email or SMS, reused across accounts,

items.title: Ozempic Maker Novo Nordisk Says Hackers Breached IT Systems
items.description: The pharmaceutical giant says the attackers gained access to personal data stored on the compromised systems. The post Ozempic Maker Novo Nordisk Says Hackers Breached IT Systems appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: French Government Messaging Platform Breached by Mysterious ‘Misere’ Hacker
items.description: French officials say roughly 73,000 government accounts were affected, while the threat actor claims to have stolen messages and user data from the sovereign Tchap platform. The post French Government Messaging Platform Breached by Mysterious ‘Misere’ Hacker appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: 152 Chrome Wallpaper Extensions with 105K Installs Linked to Adware and Fake Traffic
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a network of 152 Google Chrome extensions that act as new tab live wallpaper add-ons to distribute a potentially unwanted program (PUP) family. The cluster spans 38 separate Chrome Web Store publisher accounts and three brand backends: tabplugins[.]com, yowgames[.]com, and chromewallpaper[.]com. They have been collectively installed 105,000 times. The

items.title: ShinyHunters Claims Council of Europe Hack
items.description: The extortion group threatens to leak 297 GB of data allegedly stolen from the Council of Europe, including employee personal information. The post ShinyHunters Claims Council of Europe Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Popular WordPress Plugin Scripts Tampered to Plant Hidden Backdoors on Sites
items.description: An attacker tampered with trusted JavaScript files used by WordPress sites running PushEngage, OptinMonster, and TrustPulse, turning those files into a way to break into the sites. When a site administrator was logged in as the file loaded, the code created an admin account under the attacker's control and installed a hidden plugin that opened a way back in. Ordinary visitors did not trigger it

items.title: FBI, Google Dismantle ‘Outsider Enterprise’ Phishing Service
items.description: The platform used more than 9,000 phishing sites, stealing nearly 4 million credit cards and causing roughly $1.9 billion in losses. The post FBI, Google Dismantle ‘Outsider Enterprise’ Phishing Service appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Maine Disables Data Breach Portal Due to Fake Submissions
items.description: Someone posted fake VRChat and Discord data breach reports on the system, prompting the Maine AG to take action. The post Maine Disables Data Breach Portal Due to Fake Submissions appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: Sniper Dz Scams Target MENA Users via Fake Facebook Offers and Browser Alerts
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of fraudulent activity targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa by employing various fraudulent Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, and trusted organizations. "These accounts promoted fake offers, including free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs," Group-IB

items.title: Palo Alto Warns of Active Exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN Flaw
items.description: Palo Alto Networks has revealed that it has observed "active exploitation" of a recently disclosed PAN-OS vulnerability by an unknown threat actor to obtain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS score: 7.8), an authentication bypass flaw affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS software that could be exploited by bad

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: NPM 12 Will Change Script Execution Behavior to Prevent Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: By default, npm install will no longer execute scripts from dependencies, unless explicitly allowed. The post NPM 12 Will Change Script Execution Behavior to Prevent Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ShinyHunters Uses Oracle Zero-Day to Rampage Higher Ed
items.description: A major bug in Oracle's ERP software disproportionately affected American universities, and hackers have capitalized by stealing gobs of data.

items.title: Claude Fable 5 Doesn't Change the Mythos Security Story
items.description: Stay cool: Mythos 5 is an upgrade over Mythos Preview while Fable 5 is Mythos "made safe for general use," Anthropic explains.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9970, (Fri, Jun 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Phishing Attack Volume Down 20%, But Risk Still Rising
items.description: Hackers are valuing quality over quantity, using AI to upgrade their phishing attacks rather than multiply them.

items.title: Max-Severity Ivanti Flaw Exploited 24 Hours After Disclosure
items.description: Initial methods suggest attackers had likely mapped out Ivanti's asset landscape upfront and acted quickly once the exploit became public.

items.title: Segmentation Works for OT If Operators Are Paying Attention
items.description: Even the best segmentation strategy will fall apart without constant oversight and disciplined operations.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9968, (Thu, Jun 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
How has use of framing protection security headers changed in the past 3 years?, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: Back in 2023, I wrote a diary[1] discussing how commonly X-Frame-Options and CSP headers containing the frame-ancestors directive were used on 1 million most popular domains on the internet (based on the Tranco list[2]), and how they were set. Given that three years have passed since then, I thought it might be interesting to repeat the analysis and see what â€“ if anything â€“ has changed in the meantime.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 10th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9966, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-06-07, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: This diary continues the Internet Storm Center&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s tracking of the TeamPCP supply chain campaign, first documented in the SANS white paper When the Security Scanner Became the Weapon and most recently in the handler diary Activity Through 2026-05-24. Since that update, the story moved into two new places: the United States government, which formally caught up to the campaign, and the wider population of attackers now wielding the Mini Shai-Hulud framework that TeamPCP open-sourced last month.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9962, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
